topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Dr. Albright was leaving. The subject line was terse: "Transition Update." I stared at the screen, my jaw tightening until my molars ached. It wasn't just a departure; it felt like the entire foundation of my academic year had been ripped out from under me. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet library cubicle. My hands were shaking, a tremor that had nothing to do with caffeine withdrawal. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space between the shelves, my breath coming in short, ragged gasps. Every word Dr. Albright had ever said—every encouraging nod, every pointed critique—now seemed coated in a bitter, acidic residue. I wanted to scream, to dismantle the very idea of this sudden void, but all that escaped was a low, guttural sound trapped in my throat.

[story 2]

I found myself staring out the window of the lecture hall, watching the autumn leaves spiral down onto the quad. The news about Professor Davies’ resignation had hit me with a sickening lurch in my stomach. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of my desk. It was unfair. After months of grueling revisions, after countless late nights fueled by sheer stubbornness, this was the moment—the culmination of everything—and now the gatekeeper was walking away. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum sounding deafening. I walked toward the door, my steps heavy and deliberate, each movement radiating a coiled tension. When I finally reached the hallway, I didn't bother to knock; I just yanked the door open, needing to feel the cold, sharp air on my face, needing to channel this churning, hot pressure inside into some physical release.

[story 3]

"So, he's gone." The words tasted like ash in my mouth. I reread the university announcement three times, my eyes blurring over the official typeface. A deep, internal churning began in my core, a hot, relentless pressure building behind my sternum. I walked back to my desk, not sitting down, but leaning heavily against the surface, my shoulders hunched up toward my ears. My breathing became shallow, quick, as if I were trying to suck in enough air to choke on. I picked up my pen, intending to write, but the ink blurred into ugly, meaningless smudges on the page. Why did this have to happen *now*? The sheer injustice of it made my vision narrow, focusing only on the infuriating emptiness where guidance used to reside. I squeezed my eyes shut, fighting the urge to clench my fists so tightly my nails dug crescent moons into my palms.
